text: Despite the prevalence of patriarchal values in the Batak Toba culture of North Sumatra, Indonesia, Batak Toba women still endure male domination from their husbands or their male relatives. They are only required to serve as subservient Parhobas in all family events. If Ivone Gebara’s thought was developed further in the context of talking about gender equality by empowering women and through open human values discussion, certainly all individuals will undergo a personal and social change that is inclusive, just and sustainable. According to Liberation Theology, which is an underlying approach to Ivone Gebara’s theological thinking, all efforts must be made to relate the Christian faith with the reality of the oppressed communities, including through expressing solidarity towards the poor and the oppressed. This study is a literature review study of several latest articles related to Gebara’s thought. Gebara is a woman theologian who critically analyses the society and the Church in light of the patriarchal domination and argues that the religion must undergo a critical study in an inclusive manner, through an interpretation of women’s lives to be able to promote gender equality. Gebara’s ideas are considered to be able to enrich the public discourse and to be used as an educational material and instrument of feminist awareness to bring about change in society in the context of Batak Toba society. Developing Ivone Gebara’s thoughts in the context of Indonesia, especially in the Batak Toba culture is believed to be able to support the struggle to promote gender equality through the enhancement of religion-based rules in the community and through fostering discussion towards gender equality on the basis of different forms of societal culture.
